First, the state is a set of institutions that cannot, qua institutional ensemble, exercise power.

Second, political forces do not exist independently of the state: they are shaped in part through its forms of representation, its internal structure, and its forms of intervention.

Third, state power is a complex social relation that reflects the changing balance of social forces in a determinate conjuncture.

Fourth, state power is capitalist to the extent that it creates, maintains, or restores the conditions required for capital accumulation in a given situation and it is non-capitalist to the extent that these conditions are not realised.

The first guideline involves an institutional definition of the state and entails no guarantees about its capitalist nature. For, if the state is not to be constituted a priori as capitalist or viewed as a simple instrument, it is necessary to define it in terms that involve only contingent references (if any) to the capitalist mode of production and/or the economically dominant class. In short, while it may well be necessary to refer to the specific qualities of the CMP and the relations between capital and wage-labour in discussing the state in capitalist societies, it is important to avoid any relation of logical entailment such that the existence of the CMP necessarily implies the capitalist character of the state apparatus and/or state power. Thus it should be emphasised that, while capitalist relations of production may be a basic condition of existence of the so-called ‘particularisation’ or structural differentiation of the state form, it does not follow that the state form is therefore essentially capitalist nor that it will necessarily serve in turn to reproduce capitalist relations of production. This is especially important to emphasise because the state is located on the terrain of the social formation rather than the pure CMP and is also the site of non-class relations as well as class relations.

The most appropriate way of avoiding these problems is to adopt an institutional definition of the state. In this context it is acceptable to define its institutional boundaries in terms of the legal distinction between ‘public’ and ‘private’ provided that one does not treat the state as an originating subject endowed with an essential unity nor neglect the role of private institutions and forces in securing political domination. For much of the controversy about the state makes sense only on the false assumption that the state has a definite unity because it is a subject or because it performs a specific, pre-given function or functions in capitalist reproduction. But there are no valid grounds for presupposing the essential class unity of the state and various arguments suggest that it is necessarily fragmented and fissured. The state comprises a plurality of institutions (or apparatuses) and their unity, if any, far from being pregiven, must be constituted politically. Indeed, not only has this been emphasised in (neo-) Gramscian analyses of hegemony (above all in the later work of Nicos Poulantzas), it is also stressed in some studies in ‘form derivation’ in so far as they treat the institutional separation of the state and the economy as problematic and as conditional on the continuing imposition through struggle of various fetishised forms of action. More generally it should be noted that this suggestion, precisely because it involves no arguments about the necessary form and functions of the state, can accommodate changes in these variables and their articulation without recourse to reductionist or essentilist arguments. Indeed, if we proceed from abstract arguments about the institutional separation of the economic and political under capitalism to more concrete and complex analyses of these regions, concern with the exact institutional forms of political representation and state intervention is crucial.”翻译为中文

显而易见的是，反演涉及假设的形成，这些假设需要经过严格评估。在这样的批评中有三个相互关联的时刻：形式上的、实质上的和经验上的。然而，由于我们将在后续部分讨论国家理论的实质方面，而其他理论体系的实质标准在这里不相关，我们将专注于评估假设的形式和经验方面。

从形式上讲，我们已经强调，一个合理的假设必须完全解释解释对象，即必须具体说明“偶然必要”的条件，这些条件加在一起，完全解释了在其定义的抽象水平和复杂程度上解释对象的存在。假设还应当能够适当地扩展到较低的抽象水平和/或更高的复杂程度。此外，它所指定的“偶然必要”条件必须独立于待解释的现象，即假设不应该是重言式的，也不应该仅仅是一个经验概括。与这一标准相关的是，假设不应该包括一个假设演绎系统，其前提（以及结论）仅限于实际情况。最后，我们应当注意，构成假设的各个要素应当逻辑一致，且在它们来自不同分析层面时，应当是兼容的。

虽然这些形式标准可以用来评估解释或假设的形式充分性，也应当尝试评估其经验充分性。然而，由于无法直接接触到现实世界，知识总是理论、调查技术和现实本身之间复杂互动的结果。事实上，正是因为所有知识是通过复杂、过度决定的探究过程生成的，无法评估这些决定因素中每一个对所谓的“事实”形成的贡献程度，以及它们如何“准确”地代表被假定为独立于其智力和物质意义存在的现实世界。因为假设无法通过直接与现实世界的对抗来测试，经验评估涉及将假设中的命题与通过特定经验技术和程序产生的证据形式的命题进行比较。在自然科学中，这通常可以通过在受控条件下进行实验来完成；在社会科学中，我们必须依赖于历史和比较分析和/或在基本上开放的系统中进行实际干预。

在所有情况下，应当强调，这样的经验测试受到传统的、理论构建的证伪标准制约。因此，尽管两组命题之间的一定一致性可以被视为对所研究假设经验充分性的初步支持，但这种支持远非无可辩驳和不可争议。一致性可能是虚假的、随机的、偶然的或其他机制的效果。同样，虽然不一致性可能提供对假设经验充分性的初步证伪，但这并不意味着所测试的理论一定是不充分或错误的。相反，它提出了如何通过进一步理论工作修复不一致性的问题。如果目前无法将其追溯到证据产生中涉及的理论和/或技术，就有必要考虑是否可以通过修改或重新组合不同要素在所测试的理论或理论的条款内解决不一致性。如果不能这样做，应当尝试用可以解决这些矛盾或通过重新定义初始解释对象来消除它们的替代理论替换所测试的理论或理论。（在讨论反演、假设形成和经验测试时，我们广泛参考了Bhaskar, 1978年，和Sayer, 1979年，以及在较小程度上参考了della Volpe, 1969年，和Laclau, 1975年）。

在对关联方法的一般评述中，我故意避免对特定实质命题的任何承诺。理论分析是一个持续的过程，涉及对现有理论、概念、技术和证据的不断更新转化，避免在知识论和方法论问题的概述中预先关闭对实质性问题的后续讨论是重要的。当然，后者也可能转变。但在发展实质理论时，我们需要暂时悬置这些问题的不信任，因此有必要为后续部分建立一些知识论和方法论规范。特别是，我强调实质理论应能够理解“实际具体”作为多重决定的复杂综合体。我认为，这意味着关联方法是理论构建和表述的最适合基础，只要它与对经验评估以及形式和实质批评的承诺结合在一起。通过结合这些不同形式的批评，理论进步发生在当前理论“原材料”、调查技术和理论活动的社会关系确定的限制和机会之内，并受到一般经济、政治和意识形态形势的过度决定。

说到这里，让我们现在考虑一些更实质性的问题。

什么是需要理论化的？

我们现在可以转向评估马克思主义国家理论的实质标准问题。在这里我们必须注意，没有任何理由期望纯粹的马克思主义方法能够完全分析国家。相反。虽然马克思主义作为一个理论方法关注生产关系的分析、它们的各种存在条件以及它们对其他社会关系的影响，但国家位于社会形态的领域上，这包括的不仅仅是经济关系及其存在条件。这并不是说不能检查国家的经济存在条件，以及国家作为一个整体如何影响经济关系的再生产。这是说，对国家、其各种存在条件及其对其他社会关系的影响的分析将包括远不止经济关系和阶级力量的问题。尽管如此，我可以提供一些应当由充分的马克思主义方法满足的标准，而且还可以添加更多标准以确保更全面的理论调查。

因此，对资本主义社会中国家的马克思主义分析将在以下方面被认为是充分的：（a）它基于资本主义作为一种生产方式的特定品质，并且还允许对CMP与其他社会和/或私人劳动关系的结合的影响进行分析，（b）它在资本积累过程中赋予阶级力量间互动的中心作用，（c）它建立了社会的政治和经济特征之间的关系，而不是将其中一个简化为另一个，或视为完全独立和自主的，（d）它允许历史和民族差异在资本主义社会形态中国家形式和功能中的体现，（e）它不仅允许根据和/或与非资本主义生产关系相关的阶级力量的影响，还允许非阶级力量的影响（参见Jessop, 1977，第353页）。提出这些实质标准的理由应该从我们对三大战后马克思主义国家理论学派的批评中显而易见。在建议满足这些标准的最合适方法时，我们将对它们的合理性提供评论。因此，让我们现在提出一些指导方针，用于构建对资本主义社会中国家的充分的马克思主义描述。

首先，国家是一系列机构的集合，它们作为机构集合，不能行使权力。

其次，政治力量并非独立于国家存在：它们部分通过国家的代表形式、内部结构和干预形式塑造。

第三，国家权力是一种复杂的社会关系，反映了在特定形势中社会力量变化的平衡。

第四，国家权力在某种程度上是资本主义的，因为它创造、维持或恢复在给定情况下资本积累所需的条件；它在某种程度上是非资本主义的，因为这些条件没有实现。

第一个指导方针涉及国家的机构定义，并不保证其资本主义性质。因为，如果国家不被预先构建为资本主义的，也不被视为简单的工具，就有必要以仅涉及与资本主义生产方式和/或经济主导阶级有偶然联系（如果有的话）的条款来定义它。简而言之，虽然在讨论资本主义社会中的国家时，可能确实需要参考CMP的特定品质和资本与雇佣劳动之间的关系，但重要的是避免任何逻辑必然性关系，即CMP的存在必然意味着国家机构和/或国家权力的资本主义性质。因此，应强调，虽然资本主义生产关系可能是所谓的国家形式“特殊化”或结构分化的基本存在条件之一，但这并不意味着国家形式因此本质上是资本主义的，也不意味着它将必然反过来再生产资本主义生产关系。特别需要强调这一点，因为国家位于社会形态的领域，而不是纯粹的CMP，并且也是非阶级关系以及阶级关系的场所。

避免这些问题的最合适方法是采用国家的机构定义。在这种情况下，以“公共”与“私人”之间的法律区分定义其机构边界是可以接受的，前提是一个人不将国家视为一个拥有本质统一性的起源主体，也不忽视私人机构和力量在确保政治统治中的作用。因为关于国家的大部分争议只有在错误假设国家具有明确统一性的基础上才有意义，因为它是一个主体，或因为它在资本主义再生产中执行特定的预先给定的功能或功能。但没有有效的理由预设国家的本质上的阶级统一性，并且各种论点表明它必然是支离破碎和裂痕的。国家包括多个机构（或装置）的复数形式，其统一性（如果有的话）远非先验给定，必须通过政治手段构建。事实上，不仅在（新）葛兰西对霸权的分析中得到了强调（尤其是在尼科斯·普朗查斯的后期作品中），而且在一些“形式衍生”研究中也得到了强调，因为它们将国家和经济的机构分离视为有问题的，并且受到通过斗争强加的各种拟人化行动形式的持续施加的条件。更一般地说，应当注意，这一建议，正是因为它不涉及关于国家必要形式和功能的论点，可以在不诉诸于还原论或本质主义论点的情况下适应这些变量及其关联的变化。确实，如果我们从关于在资本主义下经济和政治的机构分离的抽象论点出发，进入对这些领域的更具体和复杂的分析，对政治代表的确切机构形式和国家干预的关注至关重要。

请将“The strength of this first guideline can be demonstrated most easily by considering the implications of rejecting it. Thus to treat the state as a real (as opposed to formal, legal) subject with a pregiven unity is to exclude from view political struggles within and between state apparatuses as well as the effects of its institutional structure on the constitution and conduct of political struggles beyond the state. Likewise to endow the state with an essential unity or inevitable bourgeois character is to engage in crude reductionism and to suggest that the only valid form of socialist political struggle is one concerned to smash the existing state apparatus. Moreover, if one adopts such an essentialist position or simply argues that all institutions of class domination (or, pace the early Poulantzas, all institutions securing social cohesion in a class-divided society) should be included in one’s definition of the state, it becomes impossible to differentiate between democratic and non-democratic forms of domination and to discuss the effects of changes in the overall institutional boundaries of the state. All states in capitalist societies would be reduced to the indifferent status of ‘dictatorships of the bourgeoisie’ and there would be no reason to prefer one or another state form or form of regime. (This said, it must be admitted that the legal distinction between ‘public’ and ‘private’ is in one sense ‘tainted’: for it is a distinction internal to bourgeois law and it develops most fully only with the dominance of the liberal, laissez-faire stage of capitalism. Nonetheless its effects are not reducible to its conditions of existence - any more than the conditions of existence of capitalist relations of production thereby become essentially bourgeois in nature. Where this juridical approach to a definition is considered inappropriate, it might be possible to define the state in terms of its monopoly over coercion. But even here the same problem occurs at one remove: for this monopoly is itself typically constitutionalised and provides one of the grounds for differentiating the ‘public’ from the ‘private’. In addition there is no guarantee that the state can successfully maintain this monopoly in all circumstances. In any event the arguments in favour of some form of institutional definition as an initial point of reference for a study of the state should become clearer as we consider our other guidelines.)

The second guideline must be interpreted in association with an institutional definition of the state. Together they exclude an instrumentalist approach that reduces the state to a neutral mechanism of power. For, although the state should not be seen as a subject capable of exercising power, it does have unequal and asymmetrical effects on the ability of different social forces to realise their interests through political action. This means that the state is not and cannot be a neutral instrument. It does not exclude the view that the state (or, more precisely, its various institutional means and resources) can be used: this, after all, is the very rationale behind the struggle for state power. But the structures of political representation and state intervention involve differential access to the state apparatuses and differential opportunities to realise specific effects in the course of state intervention. It is for this reason that a principal aim of class struggle must be the reorganisation of the state apparatuses in order to redefine their accessibility and/or their instrumentality for various class forces. This argument is reinforced through a consideration of the way in which political forces are themselves constituted at least in part through the institutional structure of the state and the effects of state intervention. Prima facie support for this approach is provided by a brief reflection on the growth of corporatism at the expense of parliamentarism, struggles over electoral reform, laws on secondary picketing, the role of legislation and state policy-making in the interpellation of particular subjects and the constitution of their interests, and so forth. This means that classes should not be seen as already-constituted political forces which exist outside and independently of the state and which are able to manipulate it as a simple, passive instrument. For, although classes as objective categories of economic agents are defined at the level of the relations of production, their political weight depends on the forms of organisation and means of intervention through which their economic (and other) class interests are expressed. In this sense one can say, following Przeworski, that political class struggle is first of all a struggle to constitute classes as political forces before it is a struggle between classes (see Przeworski, 1977, pp. 371-373 and, in addition, pp. 242-244 below). In this context the nature of the state is an important variable. Similar considerations apply to other political forces besides capital and wage-labour - whether primarily class forces or non-class forces (see below). Thus the state should be investigated as a system of political domination whose structure has a definite effect on class struggle through its role in determining the balance of forces and the forms of political action.

The third guideline implies a firm rejection of any and all attempts to distinguish between ‘state power’ and ‘class power’ (whether as separate descriptive concepts or alternative principles of explanation) in so far as such attempts establish this distinction by constituting the state itself as a power subject and/or deny the continuing class struggle within the state as well as beyond it. This is not to argue that state power is reducible to class power nor is it to deny the influence of political categories such as the military, bureaucrats, or parliamentary deputies. It is to argue that any attempt to differentiate among types of power should be made in terms of their institutional mediation and/or their social bases. In this sense we could distinguish state power from economic power in terms of its sui generis institutional mediation or distinguish ‘bureaucratic despotism’ from the ‘bourgeois democratic republic’ in terms of their respective social bases. A fortiori we could also distinguish among forms of state power in terms of the specific branches of the state system through which they are mediated as well as among different forms of democratic republic according to their specific configurations of social support. In denying the validity of the distinction between ‘state power’ and ‘class power’ in these terms we are not denying that the role of the state as a system of political class domination may sometimes be secondary to its role as a system of official domination over ‘popular-democratic’ forces. For this latter distinction does not involve the illegitimate assumption that the state is a power subject but merely treats it as an institutional complex conditioning and conditioned by the balance of forces. Nor do we deny that non-class forces initially located beyond the state (i.e., social categories based on the terrain of civil society as opposed to political categories and economic classes) can be involved in the exercise of state power and be affected by state intervention. For this merely indicates the complexity of the state apparatus and state power. Instead our third guideline is intended to stress that state power is a form-determined, institutionally mediated effect of the balance among all forces in a given situation.

This implies that state power is an explanandum, not a principle of explanation. That is, rather than explaining specific events in terms of an immediate, unexplicated exercise of state power, it is necessary to problematise the effects of state intervention and examine their specific institutional mediation and social bases. It also means that power must be seen in conjunctural, relational terms rather than as a fixed sum of resources which can be appropriated by one social force to the exclusion of others. In short our third guideline is intended to redirect studies of state power away from simple instrumentalist or subjectivist views of the state to a complex investigation of state power as a form-determined condensation of social forces in struggle.

The fourth guideline in turn implies a radical displacement of analytical focus from the search for guarantees that the state apparatus and its functions are necessarily capitalist in all aspects to a concern with the many and varied contingent effects of state power on accumulation in determinate conjunctures. These effects depend on a wide range of factors and cannot be reduced to a simple realisation of purported needs of capital. Indeed, since the conditions of existence of capital accumulation are neither unitary nor mutually consistent and since the course of capital accumulation is relatively open, it is imperative to specify which particular conditions contingently necessary for a given course of accumulation are being secured in what respects, over which time period, and to what extent. The very complexity and contradictoriness of these conditions of existence and the range of potentially viable paths of accumulation invalidate all attempts to suggest that the state in capitalist societies is unequivocally and universally beneficial to capital.

The guideline suggests that capital accumulation should have a dual theoretical function in an analysis of the state in capitalist societies: it is both a point of reference and a principle of explanation. We should not conflate the two nor stress one to the exclusion of the other. To treat capital accumulation solely as a principle of explanation would reduce the state to a more or less complex effect of the self-realisation of capital: to treat it solely as a point of reference would endow the state with absolute autonomy vis-à-vis the movement of capital. Instead we should examine how the particular institutional form of the state and the character of state intervention affect its ability to secure various conditions of existence of capital accumulation considered as a point of reference as well as how the nature and course of capital accumulation as form and process conditions the state apparatus and circumscribes (without fully determining) the effects of state intervention. In other words, in so far as we are dealing with the form and extent of realisation of the conditions of existence of capital accumulation, capital accumulation acts initially as a point of reference; and, in so far as we are dealing with the economic conditions of existence of the state apparatus and the economic laws delimiting the effectiveness of state intervention, capital accumulation can be considered as a complex of causal mechanisms and thus as a principle of explanation.”翻译为中文

第一个指导方针的强度可以通过考虑拒绝它的含义来最容易地展示。因此，将国家视为具有预设统一性的真实（与形式上的、法律上的相反）主体，将排除对国家机构内部和之间的政治斗争以及其制度结构对国家以外的政治斗争的构成和行为的影响的视角。同样，赋予国家本质统一性或不可避免的资产阶级特性，将导致粗糙的还原论，并暗示唯一有效的社会主义政治斗争形式是旨在粉碎现有国家机构的斗争。此外，如果一个人采纳这种本质主义立场或简单地争辩称所有阶级统治机构（或，遵循早期的普朗查斯，所有确保阶级分化社会中社会凝聚力的机构）应该包含在对国家的定义中，就不可能区分民主与非民主形式的统治，并讨论国家整体制度边界变化的影响。所有资本主义社会的国家都将被简化为“资产阶级专政”的不加区分的状态，将没有理由偏好某种国家形式或政权形式。（话虽如此，必须承认，'公共'与'私人'之间的法律区分在某种意义上是“受到污染的”：因为它是资产阶级法律内部的区分，在自由放任的资本主义阶段占主导地位时发展得最为充分。尽管如此，其影响并不可简化为其存在条件——就像生产关系的存在条件并非本质上是资产阶级性质一样。如果这种法律方法被认为不恰当，或许可以根据其对强制手段的垄断来定义国家。但即便在这里，相同的问题仍然以一种间接方式出现：因为这种垄断本身通常是宪法化的，为区分'公共'与'私人'提供了依据之一。此外，不能保证国家在所有情况下都能成功维持这种垄断。无论如何，支持采用某种形式的制度定义作为对国家研究的初始参照点的论据将随着我们考虑其他准则而变得更加明确。）

第二个指导方针必须与对国家的制度定义结合起来解释。它们共同排除了将国家简化为中性力量机制的工具论方法。因为，尽管不应将国家视为有能力行使权力的主体，但它确实对不同社会力量通过政治行动实现其利益的能力产生了不平等和不对称的影响。这意味着，国家不是也不能是中性工具。这并不排除国家（或更准确地说，其各种制度手段和资源）可以被使用的观点：毕竟，这正是争夺国家权力斗争的理由。但政治代表和国家干预的结构涉及到对国家机构的不同程度的准入和在国家干预过程中实现特定效果的不同机会。正因为如此，阶级斗争的一个主要目标必须是国家机构的重组，以重新定义它们的可访问性和/或各种阶级力量的工具性。政治力量本身至少部分通过国家的制度结构和国家干预的效果构成的考虑增强了这一论点。对这种方法的初步支持由对公司主义取代议会制的增长、选举改革斗争、二级罢工法律、立法和国家政策制定在特定主体的召唤和其利益构成中的作用等的简要反思提供。这意味着，类别不应被视为已经构成的政治力量，它们存在于国家之外且独立于国家，并能将其作为简单的、被动的工具进行操纵。因为，尽管作为经济行为主体的阶级在生产关系层面上被定义，但它们的政治影响力取决于通过其经济（及其他）阶级利益表达的组织形式和干预手段。从这个意义上说，可以根据普热沃斯基的说法，政治阶级斗争首先是一场将阶级构成为政治力量的斗争，然后才是阶级之间的斗争（参见Przeworski，1977年，第371-373页，另见下文，第242-244页）。在这个背景下，国家的性质是一个重要的变量。类似的考虑也适用于资本和雇佣劳动以外的其他政治力量——无论是主要的阶级力量还是非阶级力量（见下文）。因此，应当将国家作为一个政治统治系统进行研究，其结构通过在确定形势中决定力量平衡和政治行动形式的作用对阶级斗争产生明确影响。

第三个指导方针暗示了对区分“国家权力”和“阶级权力”（无论作为分离的描述性概念还是替代性解释原则）的所有尝试的坚定拒绝，因为这些尝试通过将国家本身构建为一个权力主体和/或否认国家内部以及国家之外的持续阶级斗争来确立这种区分。这并不是说国家权力可以简化为阶级权力，也不是否认军事人员、官僚或议会代表等政治类别的影响。这是要说，任何试图区分不同类型权力的尝试都应该根据它们的制度中介和/或社会基础来进行。从这个意义上，我们可以根据国家权力的特有制度中介将国家权力与经济权力区分开来，或根据它们各自的社会基础将“官僚专制”与“资产阶级民主共和国”区分开来。更进一步，我们还可以根据特定的国家系统分支介导它们以及根据具体的社会支持配置区分不同形式的民主共和国中的国家权力形式。否认“国家权力”与“阶级权力”之间在这些条款上的区分的有效性，并不是否认国家作为政治阶级统治系统的作用有时可能次于其作为官方统治“大众民主”力量系统的作用。因为这后一种区分并不涉及将国家非法地假设为权力主体的假设，而只是将其视为一个影响和受到力量平衡影响的制度复合体。我们也不否认最初位于国家之外的非阶级力量（即，基于民间社会领域的社会类别，而不是政治类别和经济阶级）可以参与国家权力的行使并受到国家干预的影响。因为这只是表明国家机构和国家权力的复杂性。相反，我们的第三个指导方针旨在强调国家权力是一种形式决定的、通过制度中介的、在给定情况下所有力量平衡产生的效果。

这意味着国家权力是一个待解释现象，而不是一个解释原则。也就是说，而不是用国家权力的直接、未经解释的行使来解释特定事件，有必要问题化国家干预的效果，并检查它们的具体制度中介和社会基础。这也意味着权力必须以形势化的、关系化的条款来看待，而不是作为一种固定的、可以被一个社会力量独占而排除其他力量的资源总和。简而言之，我们的第三个指导方针旨在将对国家权力的研究从简单的工具论或主体论视角转向对国家权力作为斗争中社会力量凝结的形式决定的复杂调查。

第四个指导方针反过来意味着从寻找确保国家机构及其功能在所有方面必然是资本主义性质的保证的分析焦点的根本性转变，转而关注国家权力在确定形势下对积累的许多和不同的偶然影响。这些影响取决于广泛的因素，不能简化为对所谓资本需求的简单实现。事实上，由于资本积累的存在条件既不是单一的也不是相互一致的，而资本积累的过程相对开放，因此有必要具体说明在给定积累过程中其偶然必要的特定条件以何种方面、在何种时间段和在何种程度上得到保障。这些条件的存在的复杂性和矛盾性以及积累的潜在可行路径的范围使得所有试图表明资本主义社会中的国家对资本普遍且绝对有利的企图都无效。

指导方针表明，在对资本主义社会中的国家进行分析时，资本积累应具有双重理论功能：它既是参照点也是解释原则。我们不应混淆这两者，也不应强调一个而排除另一个。将资本积累仅视为解释原则将使国家简化为资本自我实现的较为复杂的效应：将其仅视为参照点将使国家对资本运动具有绝对自主性。相反，我们应该检查国家的特定制度形式和国家干预的性质如何影响其保障作为参照点的资本积累的各种存在条件的能力，以及资本积累的性质和过程如何作为形式和过程条件国家机构，并限定（但不完全决定）国家干预的效果。换句话说，就资本积累的存在条件的形式和实现程度而言，资本积累最初作为参照点；就国家机构的经济存在条件和限定国家干预有效性的经济法则而言，资本积累可以被视为一组复杂的因果机制，因而作为解释原则。

请将“Such an approach permits analysis of the dialectic between the economic and political at the same time as it allows one to establish their ‘relative autonomy’ in accordance with the third criterion for an adequate Marxist analysis of the state (see p. 221 above). In this context, ‘relative autonomy’ is either an abstract, formal concept serving merely a diacritical function in demarcating our preferred approach from simple reductionism and/or the absolute autonomisation of different regions or else it is a concrete, descriptive concept whose content varies across conjunctures. It cannot function as a principle of explanation in its own right but is itself an explanandum in the same way as concepts such as ‘state power’. For, although many theorists invoke its ‘relative autonomy’ to explain the alleged functionality of the state for bourgeois reproduction, such an approach merely ascribes a ‘relative autonomy’ to the state in order the better to guarantee its subordination to the imperatives of capital accumulation and bourgeois political domination. The institutional separation of the state is an important element in its ‘relative autonomy’ but, far from assuring its functionality, this separation actually problematises the capacity of the state to secure the conditions necessary to bourgeois reproduction. The ‘relative autonomy’ of actual states is the complex resultant of their form(s) of separation from the economic region and civil society (in the sense of the site of ‘private’, non-economic relations), their sui generis institutional structure, their social bases of support and resistance, and the effectiveness of their policies in relation to bourgeois reproduction (or some other point of reference). To neglect this complex overdetermination of state power in favour of the essentialisation of ‘relative autonomy’ as an abstract principle of explanation is to neglect the deeply problematic functionality of the state apparatus and state power.

It is also important to recognise that the manner in which the dialectic between the economic and political is analyzed will vary with the problem under discussion. The more concrete and complex the explanandum and hence the more specific the conditions of existence to be examined, the more we must move beyond the invocation of general laws, tendencies, and counter-tendencies and the more detailed we must be about their mediations. However useful the concept of the ‘TRPF’ or that of the ‘contradiction between the socialisation of the productive forces and the private character of the relations of production’ may be in abstract analyses of the economic region and the role of the state, they are too indeterminate for an adequate explanation of actual economic and political events and it is essential to study their overdetermination in different conjunctures. Failure to consider this overdetermination in more concrete and complex situations can easily lead to a slippage from the method of articulation to that of subsumption and to a treatment of the state as essentially rather than contingently capitalist in character.

Although our comments on the fourth guideline have been addressed to the role of the state in bourgeois reproduction they are also applicable to its role in the reproduction of other social relations. Thus similar considerations would obtain in an analysis of the dialectical interaction between patriarchal domination and the form and functions of the state or an examination of the state as a site of bureaucratic despotism rather than class domination. Indeed all four of the guidelines presented above can be adopted with appropriate changes in considering other points of reference in terms of their political conditions of existence and/or their role in determining the nature and effectiveness of the state. In this respect I am in full agreement with Hindess and Hirst concerning the multiplicity of possible points of reference in social analysis and hence in refusing to privilege the reproduction of the dominant mode of production as the point of reference. Similarly, I insist on the multiplicity of possible causal mechanisms or principles of explanation and refuse to privilege economic determinations in the first, intermediate, or last instance. This is not to deny the validity of the Marxist approach to political economy or to suggest that the ending of economic exploitation is not a primary goal of socialist political struggle. It is to argue that the Marxist approach is not a self-sufficient totalising perspective and that economic exploitation cannot be the unique point of reference in social analysis. My own approach to state theory is intended to provide the means to integrate Marxist perspectives with other points of reference and principles of explanation so that concrete, complex phenomena can be adequately theorised and explained. It is for this reason that I must reject economism and class reductionism and recognise that the weight of economic determinations and class forces in an explanans must change with the nature of the explanandum. Let us now see how this approach can be developed further in the light of the above-mentioned substantive criteria.

POLITICAL REPRESENTATION AND STATE
INTERVENTION

I have argued that the state is best seen initially as an institutional ensemble and it is now time to elaborate this argument. In general terms we can say that the state is an institutional ensemble of forms of representation, internal organisation, and intervention. This implies that state forms and regime types can be distinguished in terms of the differential articulation of political representation, internal organisation, and state intervention. This approach has several advantages.

It provides a means to examine the linkages among the state, economy, and civil society in terms of the form-determined mediation of demands and support as well as the state’s form-determined role in maintaining political domination, capital accumulation, and private, non-economic forms of domination. This will prove particularly useful in examining the social bases of support for and resistance to the state. It also enables us to examine the effects of incongruence between forms of representation and intervention and/or their inadequate linkage within the state apparatus itself (e.g., the crisis of liberal parliamentarism accompanying the growth of state economic intervention). It emphasises the hybrid character of the state and thus points to the need to assess the hierarchy among different forms of representation and intervention and the role played by the internal organisation of the state in reproducing the pattern of domination and subordination among these forms. This will prove useful in considering problems of periodisation as well as in comparing ‘normal’ and ‘exceptional’ forms of state. It points to the areas where crises can occur in the state form: representation, internal unity, intervention, and the relations among these elements. Finally, in treating the state as an institutional ensemble in this way, we are also considering it as a ‘structurally selective’ system of political domination which conditions the formation of political forces and their ability to achieve specific effects through the state.

These forms can be analyzed at different levels of abstraction and complexity. At the most abstract and simple level of a Marxist analysis we can consider how the institutional separation of the economic and the political implies the possibility of divorcing political representation in formal terms from questions of economic ownership at the same time as it implies the indirect and mediated character of state intervention in the circuit of capital. Some of the abstract constraints and/or opportunities this entails have already been well rehearsed in the analyses of the ‘Staatsableitungdebatte’ and in the work of Nicos Poulantzas. More determinate analyses must specify the particular forms of representation and intervention and consider their articulation with changing balances of social force. In this context some preliminary distinctions can be introduced which may help to order subsequent analyses without suggesting that they are exhaustive or definitive. Let us begin with some forms of representation and then consider types of economic intervention.

Without claiming to cover all possible forms of representation it is still useful to distinguish among five systems relevant state intervention. Clientelism may be characterised as a form of representation based on the exchange of political support in return for the allocation of politically-mediated resources and involving a hierarchical relationship between dependent client(s) and superordinate patron(s). Corporatism involves political representation on the basis of function within the division of labour and is characterised by the formal equivalence of ‘corporations’ whose members perform substantively different functions. Parliamentarism may be defined as a form of representation based on the indirect participation of individual ‘citizens’ in policy-making through their exercise of voting and accompanying rights in relation to an elected legislature and/or political executive; it is associated with formal equality among individual ‘citizens’ and the formal freedoms necessary for its operation provide the basis for the development of pluralism as well as party organisation. Pluralism is a form of representation based on institutionalised channels of access to the state apparatuses for political forces representing interests and/or causes rooted in civil society (as opposed to function in the division of labour) and recognised as legitimate by relevant branches of the state. Raison d’etat is a limit case of intervention without formal channels of representation but it is not incompatible with informal channels of representation nor with attempts to legitimate such intervention in terms of the national or public interest (cf. the remarks in Jessop, 1979, pp. 193-201; and idem, 1980b, pp. 59-63).

These forms have definite (but not fully determinate) effects on the accessibility of the state to different political forces and also influence the way in which political forces themselves are constituted. Thus, whereas parliamentarism encourages the political fragmentation of economic categories and promotes the interpellation of those who belong to economic classes as individual citizens (cf. Poulantzas on the ‘isolation effect’), corporatism encourages the organisation of economic classes into functionally heterogeneous, interdependent, and formally equivalent groupings for whom collaboration and concertation are mutually advantageous and also discourages their organisation as polarised, antagonistic, mutually contradictory classes (cf. ‘surface form’ theorists on ‘revenue categories’). Again, whereas clientelism and pluralism promote the particularistic reproduction of specific ‘economic-corporate’ and ‘civil-corporate’ interests and thereby help to sustain a pattern of non-hegemonic, ‘transformist’ politics, parliamentarism provides a medium through which political parties can seek to mobilise political support behind an appropriate hegemonic project and thereby contribute to the consolidation of hegemony. A final example of the political effects of different forms of representation can be found in the way in which the growth of corporatism displaces the dominant field of representation to the advantage of vested economic interests at the expense of forces seeking representation through electoral channels. It is difficult to be more precise about the implications of forms of representation since they also depend on the forces in contention and the links between representation and intervention.”翻译为中文

这种方法允许同时分析经济与政治之间的辩证关系，并根据对充分的马克思主义国家分析的第三个标准（见上文第221页）建立它们的“相对自主性”。在这种语境下，“相对自主性”要么是一个抽象的、形式上的概念，仅仅起到区分我们的首选方法与简单还原论和/或不同领域的绝对自治化的区别作用；要么是一个具体的、描述性的概念，其内容因形势而异。它不能作为一个自成一体的解释原则，而是自身也是一个解释对象，与“国家权力”等概念的方式相同。因为，尽管许多理论家援引其“相对自主性”来解释国家对资产阶级再生产的所谓功能性，这种方法仅仅是将“相对自主性”归因于国家，以更好地保证其从属于资本积累和资产阶级政治统治的必要性。国家的制度分离是其“相对自主性”的一个重要元素，但远非确保其功能性，这种分离实际上使国家保障资产阶级再生产所需条件的能力成为问题。实际国家的“相对自主性”是它们与经济领域和民间社会（作为“私人”、非经济关系的场所）分离形式、独特的制度结构、支持与抵抗的社会基础，以及其政策相对于资产阶级再生产（或某些其他参照点）的有效性的复杂结果。忽视国家权力的这种复杂过度决定性，而将“相对自主性”的本质化作为一个抽象解释原则，是忽略了国家机构和国家权力的深刻问题性功能。

同样重要的是要认识到，分析经济与政治之间的辩证关系的方式将随着所讨论的问题而变化。解释对象越具体和复杂，因此需要检查的存在条件越具体，我们就越需要超越对一般法律、趋势和反趋势的援引，对其介导进行更详细的说明。尽管在对经济领域和国家角色的抽象分析中，“利润率下降趋势（TRPF）”概念或“生产力社会化与生产关系私人性之间的矛盾”概念可能有用，但对于实际经济和政治事件的充分解释来说，它们太不确定，有必要研究它们在不同形势下的过度决定性。未能在更具体和复杂的情境中考虑这种过度决定性，很容易导致从关联方法滑向归属方法，并将国家视为本质上而不是偶然地具有资本主义特征。

尽管我们对第四个指导方针的评论是针对国家在资产阶级再生产中的角色，但它们也适用于国家在再生产其他社会关系中的角色。因此，在分析父权统治与国家形式和功能之间的辩证互动，或考察国家作为官僚专制而非阶级统治场所的情形时，将会遇到类似的考虑。事实上，上文提出的所有四个准则都可以在考虑其他政治存在条件和/或在决定国家性质和有效性方面的角色时，进行适当的变更。在这方面，我完全同意Hindess和Hirst关于社会分析中可能的多个参照点及因此拒绝将主导生产方式的再生产作为参照点的观点。同样，我坚持可能的因果机制或解释原则的多样性，并拒绝在第一、中间或最后实例中给予经济决定因素特权。这并不是否定马克思主义政治经济学方法的有效性，也不是暗示结束经济剥削不是社会主义政治斗争的主要目标。这是要认为马克思主义方法不是一个自给自足的全面视角，经济剥削不能是社会分析中唯一的参照点。我自己对国家理论的方法旨在提供手段，将马克思主义视角与其他参照点和解释原则结合起来，以便充分理论化和解释具体、复杂的现象。正因为此，我必须拒绝经济决定论和阶级还原论，并认识到经济决定因素和阶级力量在解释中的重要性必须随着解释对象的性质而变化。让我们现在看看如何根据上述实质标准进一步发展这种方法。

政治代表与国家干预

我认为国家最初最好被视为一个机构集合，现在是时候详细阐述这个论点了。通常来说，我们可以说国家是政治代表、内部组织和干预形式的机构集合。这意味着可以根据政治代表、内部组织和国家干预的不同关联来区分国家形式和政权类型。这种方法有几个优点。

它提供了一种检查国家、经济和民间社会之间的联系的手段，以及国家在维护政治统治、资本积累和私人非经济形式的统治方面的形式决定的角色。这对于检查支持和反对国家的社会基础特别有用。它还使我们能够检查代表形式和干预形式之间的不一致或它们在国家机构内部的联系不足（例如，国家经济干预增长伴随的自由主义议会制危机）的影响。它强调了国家的混合特性，从而指出了评估不同代表形式和干预形式之间的层级关系以及国家内部组织在再现统治和从属模式中所起作用的必要性。这对于考虑时期划分问题以及比较“正常”和“异常”形式的国家将是有用的。它指出了国家形式可能发生危机的领域：代表、内部统一、干预以及这些要素之间的关系。最后，以这种方式将国家视为一个机构集合，我们也将其视为一个“结构性选择性”的政治统治系统，它影响政治力量的形成及其通过国家实现特定效果的能力。

这些形式可以在不同的抽象和复杂层面上进行分析。在马克思主义分析的最抽象和简单层面上，我们可以考虑经济和政治的制度分离意味着将政治代表在形式上从经济所有权问题中分离的可能性，同时这也意味着国家干预资本循环的间接性和中介性。这种分离所涉及的一些抽象约束和/或机会已经在“Staatsableitungdebatte”的分析和尼科斯·普朗查斯的工作中得到了充分说明。更具体的分析必须具体说明代表和干预的特定形式，并考虑它们与社会力量变化平衡的关联。在这个背景下，可以引入一些初步区分，这些区分可能有助于为后续分析提供秩序，而不暗示它们是详尽无遗或定论的。让我们从一些代表形式开始，然后考虑经济干预的类型。

虽然没有声称涵盖所有可能的代表形式，但仍然有用的是区分五种与国家干预相关的系统。客户主义可被视为一种基于政治支持交换以换取政治调解资源分配的代表形式，涉及从属客户和上级赞助人之间的层级关系。企业主义涉及基于劳动分工中功能的政治代表，其特征是“公司”之间的形式等价，这些公司的成员执行实质上不同的功能。议会制可以被定义为一种基于个体“公民”通过对民选立法机构和/或政治执行机构的投票和伴随权利的间接参与进行政策制定的代表形式；它与个体“公民”之间的形式平等以及其运作所需的形式自由相关联，为多元主义以及党派组织的发展提供了基础。多元主义是一种基于制度化渠道的代表形式，这些渠道为政治力量提供了进入国家机构的途径，这些力量代表根植于民间社会（与劳动分工中的功能相对）的利益和/或事业，并被国家的相关部门认为是合法的。国家理由是一种不涉及形式代表渠道的干预极限案例，但它并不与非正式代表渠道不相容，也不与试图以国家或公共利益的名义为此类干预合理化的尝试不相容（参见Jessop, 1979年，第193-201页；以及同上，1980b年，第59-63页）。

这些形式对不同政治力量进入国家的可及性产生了明确的（但不完全确定的）影响，也影响了政治力量自身的构成方式。因此，与议会制鼓励将经济类别政治碎片化并促使那些属于经济阶级的人作为个体公民进行政治召唤（参见普朗查斯关于“孤立效应”）相反，企业主义鼓励将经济阶级组织为功能上异质、相互依赖和形式上等价的群体，对于这些群体而言，协作和协商是相互有利的，也不鼓励将它们组织为两极分化的、对立的、相互矛盾的阶级（参见“表面形式”理论家关于“收入类别”）。再次，客户主义和多元主义促进了特定的“经济-企业”和“民间-企业”利益的局部性再生产，从而帮助支持了非霸权的、“变形主义”政治格局，而议会制提供了一个媒介，通过该媒介，政党可以寻求在适当的霸权项目背后动员政治支持，从而有助于霸权的巩固。不同代表形式的政治影响的最后一个例子可以在企业主义增长将主要代表领域转移至有利于既得经济利益而损害通过选举渠道寻求代表的力量的方式中找到。无法更准确地说明代表形式的影响，因为它们还取决于争议中的力量以及代表与干预之间的联系。

请将“In this context it is important to stress that an adequate account of the state must go beyond the forms of representation and intervention to include the internal organisation of the state apparatus itself. For otherwise the state will appear simply as a ‘black box’ inside which external demands and support are translated into specific policies that are then directed outwards. This ‘black box’ view is misleading not only because representation occurs within all parts of the state system but also because intervention can be directed inwards as well as outwards. At the same time it must be emphasised that the state is not exhausted by the forms of representation and intervention that link it to the economy and civil society: it also involves sui generis forms of organisation concerned with the reproduction of the state apparatus itself as a system of political domination. This requires not only the mobilisation of resources for the continued operation of the state (such as finance, personnel, means of administration) but also the formal and substantive coordination of its different branches and activities. The unity of the state apparatus - both as an institutional ensemble and as an organ of class domination - is a continuing problem.

The formal unity of the state as an institutional ensemble is typically related to the growth of bureaucratisation. This involves not only the formation of a special category of career officials separated from ownership of the means of administration but also their subordination to formal rules of legal and financial accountability in a hierarchical chain of command linking different levels and branches of the state. However this formal unity depends on the unity of the political executive at the top of the chain of command and can also be circumscribed or undermined through the resistance or non-compliance of officials at different levels or branches of the bureaucratic system. Moreover, although bureaucratic forms are appropriate to the execution of general laws or policies in accordance with the rule of law, they are less suited to ad hoc, discretionary forms of intervention (cf. the excursus on Claus Offe above; and my remarks below). Thus the transition from liberal through simple monopoly to state monopoly capitalism exacerbates the contradiction between the bureaucratic preconditions for the formal unity of the state system and the substantive efficacy of policies oriented to accumulation. This can be seen in the expansion of quasi-nongovernmental organisations (or ‘quangos’) charged with substantive support and facilitation of economic and social activities and/or with imperative or concertative direction of these activities and given much greater freedom of manoeuvre than the ministries and departments which remain formally within the bureaucratic chain of command. This expansion poses major problems even in defining the formal boundaries of the state as an institutional ensemble (on the British case, for example, see Hood et al., 1978) and also threatens the substantive unity of the state through its potential for clientelistic degeneration and the pursuit of particular ‘economic-corporate’ demands. This suggests the need for the bureaucratic mechanism to be complemented through an overarching political executive and/or cross-cutting networks that can coordinate the activities of different parts of the state. But the substantive unity of the political executive and/or networks of this kind depends in turn on their commitment to a ‘hegemonic project’ able to harmonise particular interests with a specific set of ‘national-popular’ goals (see below).

In this context it is important to note how the articulation of the various branches and departments of the state system (including bodies such as ‘quangos’) contributes to the structural determination of class hegemony. For, in addition to the general periodisation of state forms and its implications for the long-term hegemony of competitive, monopoly, or state monopoly fractions, it is also necessary to consider how the relative dominance of particular departments or ministries can underwrite the hegemony of a given class fraction. Thus the dominant role of the Treasury-Bank of England nexus in Britain is an important element in the structural determination of the hegemony of banking capital; and that of the Ministry of International Trade and Industry plays a similar role on behalf of industrial capital in Japan. This structural dominance needs to be combined with the successful propagation of a ‘hegemonic project’ for the structurally privileged fraction to become truly hegemonic but, in the absence of this, state structures can nonetheless undermine the pursuit of a project favourable to another class or class fraction. This can be seen in Labour Government’s failure to pursue its project of industrial modernisation and economic planning during its 1964-70 administration. For, although the incoming Labour administration established a new planning ministry favourable to industrial capital in the form of the Department of Economic Affairs and also undertook other initiatives to promote industrial reorganisation, the Treasury and Bank of England remained dominant and used their fiscal, expenditure, and monetary powers to turn the economic crisis to the advantage of banking capital (for a more detailed account, including reference to other causal factors, see Jessop, 1980a, pp. 38-47 and passim). In turn this implies that a long-term shift in hegemony requires not only a new ‘hegemonic project’ but also the reorganisation of the state system as a whole.

The internal structure of the state is also crucial in considering ‘normal’ and ‘exceptional’ regimes. For, whereas normal states can be categorised in terms of the relative dominance of different channels of democratic representation (clientelist, corporatist, parliamentary, and pluralist), exceptional states can be distinguished in terms of the relative dominance of different parts of the state system (such as the military, bureaucracy, political police, fascist party, and so forth). This is not to suggest that one can ignore the relative dominance of different parts of the apparatus of administration and intervention in normal states nor to suggest that exceptional states function without any channels of representation. It is to emphasise that normal and exceptional states can only be differentiated through a rigorous analysis of their institutional structure as well as their social bases.

The third aspect of institutional structure to be considered here is the nature of state intervention. For, just as there are different forms of representation, there are also different types of intervention. In the field of political economy we can distinguish among the following basic forms. In the case of formal facilitation the state maintains the general external conditions of capitalist production: these include a formally rational monetary system, a formally rational legal system, and a formally rational system of administration. To perform this facilitative role alone implies that capitalist production itself operates in a self-expanding, selfequilibrating manner through the profit-and-loss system resulting from laissez- faire and free competition. To the extent that this is not the case, some other form(s) of intervention will be required. In the case of substantive facilitation the state reproduces certain general conditions of production within capitalism, i.e., conditions whose provision is essential for the majority of individual capitals to continue production. The most general condition of this kind is provision of labour-power since it is not produced in the enterprise itself but is bought into the labour process as a simple, non-capitalist (or fictitious) commodity in exchange for wages (cf. Aumeeruddy et al., 1978, and de Brunhoff, 1976). In addition it may prove necessary for the state to supply means of production which have a general significance for capital, e.g., infrastructure, energy supplies, transport, basic research and development, economic statistics. In the case offormal support the state alters the general external conditions of production in a particularistic manner and/or establishes external conditions favourable to particular capitals. This can be accomplished through the introduction of substantively rational criteria into the legal framework, through modifications in the financial costs of specific economic activities, and through particularistic administrative measures. It should be noted that the state intervenes indirectly in offering formal support, i.e., through the mediation of law, money, and administrative measures; and that it is left to market forces to determine whether these changes are exploited by economic agents who remain formally free and autonomous. Among these measures could be included changes in competition policy, company law, investment allowances, regional employment premia, and the conditions of access to corporatist decision-making bodies. In contrast substantive support involves the direct allocation of particular conditions of production to particular economic agents rather than leaving it to the autonomous choice of market forces which agents, if any, benefit from state action. Measures of this kind could include licenses, monopolies, state credit, state sponsorship, and so forth. Although the core activities in these types of support are distinct, there is some ambiguity at the margins.

Finally, in the case of direction, the state overrides the formal freedom of economic agents and directs that they either act or refrain from acting in particular ways. Here the state no longer relies merely on facilitating or supporting market forces but intervenes to support, counteract, or modify them through restrictions on the formal autonomy and freedom of these agents. It should be noted that such restrictions on formal autonomy may promote the substantive rationality of capitalism through recognition of the substantive interdependence among economic agents and promotion of their collective interest at the cost of their particular interests. In some cases direction may be associated with the socialisation of the relations of production to match the socialisation of productive forces and thus promote rather than undermine accumulation. It should also be noted that ‘direction’ need not be secured through ‘imperative coordination’ from above: it can also be mediated through the operation of corporatist forms of concertation in which capital and/or labour cooperate with each other and with branches of the state apparatus. (For similar typologies of intervention, see Offe, 1975b, and Winkler, 1977).”翻译为中文

在这个语境中，重要的是强调，对国家的充分描述必须超越代表形式和干预形式，还要包括国家机构本身的内部组织。否则，国家将仅仅出现为一个“黑箱”，在这个黑箱内部，外部的需求和支持被转化为特定的政策，然后向外部指导。这种“黑箱”观点不仅因为所有国家系统部分都发生代表行为而具有误导性，而且因为干预不仅可以向外指导，也可以向内指导。与此同时，必须强调国家不仅仅由将其与经济和民间社会联系起来的代表形式和干预形式构成：它还涉及关注国家机构自身作为政治统治系统再生产的特有组织形式。这不仅需要动员资源以维持国家的持续运作（如财政、人员、管理手段），还需要对其不同分支和活动进行形式和实质上的协调。国家机构的统一性——无论作为一个机构集合还是作为一个阶级统治器官——都是一个持续的问题。

国家作为一个机构集合的形式统一性通常与官僚化的增长有关。这不仅涉及形成一个从管理手段所有权中分离出来的特殊职业官员类别，还涉及他们在将不同层级和部门的国家联系起来的层级指挥链中，服从法律和财政问责的形式规则的下属。然而，这种形式统一性依赖于指挥链顶端的政治行政统一性，也可能因为不同层级或官僚系统部门的官员的抵抗或不遵从而受到限制或破坏。此外，尽管官僚形式适用于根据法治执行一般法律或政策，但它们不太适用于临时性、自由裁量性的干预形式。这种从自由主义通过简单垄断到国家垄断资本主义的转变加剧了国家体系的形式统一性的官僚前提条件与面向积累的政策的实质效力之间的矛盾。可以从准非政府组织（或“准官方机构”）的扩张中看到这一点，这些机构负责对经济和社会活动提供实质性支持和促进，和/或对这些活动进行强制或协调指导，并被赋予比留在官僚指挥链内的部委更大的自由裁量权。这种扩张即使在定义国家作为一个机构集合的形式边界上也构成了重大问题（例如，参见对英国案例的研究，Hood等，1978） ，并通过其对客户主义式腐化的潜力和追求特定的“经济-企业”需求，也威胁到国家的实质统一性。这表明需要通过一个总体性的政治行政和/或横穿不同国家部分的网络，来补充官僚机制，以协调国家不同部分的活动。但这种政治行政和/或此类网络的实质统一性反过来取决于它们对能够协调特定利益与特定“民族-大众”目标集合的“霸权项目”的承诺（见下文）。

在这种背景下，重要的是注意国家系统的各个部门和机构（包括诸如“准官方机构”之类的机构）的关联如何对阶级霸权的结构决定产生贡献。因为，除了对国家形式的一般时期划分及其对竞争、垄断或国家垄断派系长期霸权的影响的考虑外，还有必要考虑特定部门或部委的相对主导地位如何支持给定阶级派系的霸权。例如，英国财政部-英格兰银行联盟的主导作用是银行资本霸权结构决定的重要因素；日本国际贸易和工业部在代表工业资本的利益方面发挥了类似作用。这种结构主导地位需要与“霸权项目”的成功传播相结合，以使得结构上获得优势的派系真正成为霸权，但即使没有这一项目，国家结构仍然可能破坏有利于另一个阶级或阶级派系的项目的追求。这可以在1964-70年工党政府未能追求其工业现代化和经济规划项目时看到。因为，尽管新上任的工党政府成立了一个有利于工业资本的新规划部门——经济事务部，并采取了其他促进工业重组的举措，但财政部和英格兰银行仍然占主导地位，并利用它们的财政、支出和货币权力将经济危机转为银行资本的有利（更详细的分析，包括对其他因果因素的参考，请参见Jessop，1980a，第38-47页及以下）。反过来，这意味着长期霸权转变不仅需要一个新的“霸权项目”，还需要对整个国家系统进行重新组织。

国家的内部结构在考虑“正常”和“异常”政权时也至关重要。因为，正常国家可以根据不同民主代表渠道（客户主义、企业主义、议会制和多元主义）的相对主导地位进行分类，而异常国家可以根据国家系统不同部分（如军队、官僚机构、政治警察、法西斯党等）的相对主导地位进行区分。这并不是说，在正常国家中可以忽视行政与干预装置不同部分的相对主导地位，也并不是说异常国家在没有任何代表渠道的情况下运作。这是为了强调，只有通过对它们的制度结构以及社会基础进行严格分析，才能区分正常和异常国家。

需要在此考虑的国家干预的第三个方面是干预的性质。因为，正如有不同的代表形式一样，也有不同类型的干预。在政治经济领域，我们可以区分以下基本形式。在形式促进的情况下，国家维护资本主义生产的一般外部条件：这些包括形式上合理的货币系统、形式上合理的法律系统和形式上合理的行政系统。单独执行这种促进作用意味着资本主义生产本身通过自由放任和自由竞争所产生的利润和损失系统以自我扩张、自我平衡的方式运作。在这种情况下，需要一些其他形式的干预。在实质性促进的情况下，国家在资本主义内再生产某些一般生产条件，即对大多数个别资本继续生产至关重要的条件的提供。最普遍的这种条件是劳动力的提供，因为它不是在企业内部生产的，而是作为一个简单的、非资本主义（或虚构的）商品通过工资交换进入劳动过程（参见Aumeeruddy等，1978年，和de Brunhoff，1976年）。此外，国家可能需要提供对资本具有普遍意义的生产资料，例如基础设施、能源供应、交通、基础研发、经济统计等。在形式支持的情况下，国家以特定的方式改变生产的一般外部条件和/或为特定资本建立有利的外部条件。这可以通过将实质合理准则引入法律框架、通过修改特定经济活动的财务成本、以及通过特定的行政措施来实现。应该注意到，国家在提供形式支持时间接干预，即通过法律、金钱和行政措施的中介；并且留给市场力量来决定这些变化是否被仍然是形式上自由和自治的经济行为者所利用。这些措施可能包括竞争政策、公司法、投资津贴、区域就业奖金以及进入企业主义决策机构的条件的变化。相比之下，实质性支持涉及直接向特定经济行为者分配特定生产条件，而不是留给市场力量的自治选择确定哪些行为者（如果有的话）受益于国家行动。此类措施可能包括许可证、垄断、国家信贷、国家赞助等。尽管这些类型支持的核心活动是不同的，但在边缘处存在一些模糊性。

最后，在指导方面，国家推翻了经济行为主体的形式自由，并指导他们以特定方式行动或不行动。这里国家不再仅仅依赖于促进或支持市场力量，而是通过限制这些行为主体的形式自主性和自由来干预支持、抵消或修改它们。应该注意到，对形式自主性的这种限制可能通过承认经济行为主体之间的实质相互依赖并促进其集体利益（而不是特定利益）来增进资本主义的实质合理性。在某些情况下，指导可能与生产关系的社会化相结合，以匹配生产力的社会化，并因此促进而非破坏积累。还应该注意，“指导”不一定通过“上级指令”的强制协调来实现：它也可以通过企业主义形式的协商操作，其中资本和/或劳动相互合作，并与国家机构的部门合作。（关于干预的类似类型分类，参见Offe，1975b，和Winkler，1977）。
